revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:女性宮家]][[zh:女性宮家]]


'''Josei no Miyake''' (女性宮家) refers to a term used in discussions about the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}} from the mid-Heisei period onward.
== Overview ==  
{{citation needed|The term "miyake" originally referred to hereditary titles within the imperial family granted to those with the right to inherit the Chrysanthemum Throne ([[皇位]]). Traditionally, this inheritance followed the principle of patrilineal descent. "Josei no Miyake" (Female Imperial Branches) became a topic of discussion during the debates about imperial succession in mid-Heisei, suggesting the possibility of female or matrilineal succession of these titles.|date=April 2022}}
The creation of "Josei no Miyake" is associated with the potential introduction of [[Female Emperors]] or matrilineal dynasties, raising legal and historical concerns.{{Sfn|櫻井よしこ・竹田恒泰・百地章|2017|p=20-24}}
== Historical Context ==  
=== Before the Edo Period ===  
Throughout Japanese history, the Chrysanthemum Throne was inherited without exception by male-line descendants of the first emperor, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}. Therefore, the miyake titles were also passed down exclusively through patrilineal descent. In cases where a direct male-line descendant was absent, the titles would pass to other male-line descendants within the imperial family.  
{{main|Hereditary Imperial Prince Families}}  
  
Some argue that this was not the establishment of a "Josei no Miyake" but rather a temporary arrangement for the management of family properties.{{Sfn|櫻井よしこ・竹田恒泰・百地章|2017|p=54}}
=== From the Meiji Era to the Shōwa Era ===  
Under the [[Imperial House Law of 1889]], Article 12 stipulated that female members of the imperial family would lose their status upon marriage to a commoner. This principle of patrilineal succession was codified, ensuring that women did not inherit miyake titles. This practice continued under the [[Imperial House Law of 1947]].  
Widows of deceased male members could act as de facto heads of their miyake, but these cases do not qualify as "Josei no Miyake."{{Cite web|url=http://mainichi.jp/select/wadai/naruhodori/news/20111126ddm003070129000chtml|title=質問なるほドリ：「女性宮家」って何？＝回答・大久保和夫|publisher=毎日新聞|date=2011-11-26}}
=== Heisei Era and Beyond ===  
{{main|Imperial Succession Controversy}}  
The issue of declining numbers of male imperial family members gained prominence in the late Heisei period. By 2005 (Heisei 17), there were no male family members under the age of 30, raising concerns about the continuity of the imperial line.  
Proposals for "Josei no Miyake" emerged during this time, though the definition of the term varied among discussions and commentators.
== Legal Perspectives ==  
The Japanese Constitution's Article 2 and the [[:en:Imperial House Law|Imperial House Law]] stipulate that the throne must be inherited through "succession." Legal and scholarly consensus generally interprets this as requiring patrilineal descent.{{Sfn|百地章|2024|p=4‐14}}
Some argue that establishing "Josei no Miyake" could violate [[Article 14 of the Constitution]], which prohibits the establishment of new nobility.{{Sfn|櫻井よしこ・竹田恒泰・百地章|2017|p=9-10}}
== Issues and Criticism ==  
The creation of "Josei no Miyake" raises questions about the legal status of spouses, children, family names, and funding. Furthermore, it could open the door to matrilineal dynasties, conflicting with constitutional interpretations of hereditary succession.{{Sfn|櫻井よしこ・竹田恒泰・百地章|2017|p=56‐57}}
== Alternatives ==  
One alternative proposal involves allowing female imperial family members to retain honorary titles after marriage. Historical precedents include Princess Nashimoto Masako's retention of the title "Princess Nashimoto" after her marriage to a Korean prince.{{Sfn|櫻井よしこ・竹田恒泰・百地章|2017|p=64-65}}
== References ==  
{{reflist}}
== Related Reading ==  
*{{Cite book|ja|author=所功|authorlink=所功|year=2012|title=皇室典範と女性宮家|publisher=勉誠出版|isbn=978-4585230151|ref=harv}}
*{{Cite book|ja|author=笠原英彦|authorlink=笠原英彦|year=2017|title=皇室がなくなる日|publisher=新潮選書|isbn=9784106037962|ref=harv}}
*{{Citation|ja|author=櫻井よしこ・竹田恒泰・百地章|date=2017-7-11|title=「女性宮家創設」ここが問題の本質だ！|publisher=明成社|ref={{SfnRef|櫻井よしこ・竹田恒泰・百地章|2017}}}}

== See Also ==  
*[[Imperial Succession Controversy]]  
*[[Female Emperor]]  
*[[Matrilineal Emperor]]  
*[[Former Imperial Family Members]]
